accepted_026.assessment:
This paper addresses a highly relevant problem—building specialist LMs from abundant generalist data using only a small amount of specialist supervision—and presents a simple, effective, and scalable method. The experimental scope is broad and the results are consistently favorable across tasks and sizes, with thoughtful ablations on clustering choices and data regimes. The main reservations are (i) incomplete baseline coverage (notably missing Moore–Lewis, kNN, hashed n-gram IS, and recent bilevel methods like BLISS), (ii) fairness issues in the DoGE comparison due to vastly different selection granularities, (iii) lack of multi-seed significance reporting, and (iv) insufficient engineering details to assess the practicality of clustering billions of windows at web scale. Despite these gaps, the paper’s core idea is compelling, the gains are consistent, and the empirical analyses are informative. With stronger baseline comparisons, clearer contamination controls, and transparent compute accounting for the selection pipeline, this work would be a strong contribution to ICLR. I lean toward acceptance after addressing these concerns.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_050.assessment:
This paper addresses an important setting—learning generative priors and solving inverse problems when only corrupted measurements are available—and contributes both a principled MRI extension of Ambient Diffusion and a practical A-DPS algorithm that leverages ambient conditional denoisers to handle different inference operators. The empirical finding that ambient models can outperform clean-trained priors in severely ill-posed regimes is compelling and of practical significance; the MRI results, in particular at R=8, are strong and backed by multiple metrics and baselines. The work is generally sound and clearly written, though the main theory is only informally presented and the A-DPS substitution could be examined more carefully (fixed-vs-random Ãtrain, sensitivity, and bias). The experimental section would be further strengthened by (i) robustness studies to noise and coil-map mismatch, (ii) stronger diffusion-based baselines (DCPS, VI with diffusion ELBO surrogates), and (iii) quantified runtime. Despite these gaps, the contributions are meaningful, well-motivated, and likely to be valuable to the community. With additional clarifications and a few targeted experiments, this paper would be a solid fit for ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_056.assessment:
This paper offers a clear, carefully executed, and timely negative result: under a widely used likelihood-based white-box optimization, universal image-only jailbreaks seldom transfer across heterogeneous VLMs, with partial exceptions among highly similar models and adjacent checkpoints. The breadth of the empirical study, the release of additional VLMs, and the connector-stage insight make this a valuable contribution to the multimodal safety literature and to our understanding of transfer in VLMs. The main limitations are the narrow attack objective, the reliance on a single external judge, and limited comparison to recent transfer-oriented multimodal attacks that leverage semantic augmentations, fusion-aware losses, or joint text+image optimization. I view the work as a strong empirical anchor—useful even if subsequent methods (e.g., fusion-layer boundary-guided, joint-modality attacks) show higher transfer under different objectives. With modest additions (multi-judge validation, clarifying threat model and constraints, deeper analysis of model similarity), this would be a solid ICLR paper. I lean toward acceptance given its scope, clarity, and practical significance for calibrating current risks and guiding future research.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_062.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and underexplored dimension of scaling laws by incorporating precision as a first-class variable and unifying training and inference-time effects. The proposed functional forms are simple, interpretable, and well supported by an extensive experimental sweep. The main findings—PTQ degradation increasing with data, a multiplicative Neff across model components, and compute-optimal precision around 7–8 bits (with a log-growth when N is fixed)—are both novel and practically consequential. The key limitations are the relatively modest scale of the main fits, lack of downstream task validation, and limited systems-level measurements; additionally, some presentation details (e.g., consistency of exponents) could be clarified. Despite these caveats, the work provides a compelling and useful step toward precision-aware scaling, filling a gap between quantization practice and scaling theory. I recommend acceptance, with the suggestion to strengthen the camera-ready with (i) clarification of the δPTQ–Neff exponent, (ii) small downstream evaluations, and (iii) brief systems measurements to triangulate the compute-optimal precision conclusions.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_068.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and challenging problem—self-supervised denoising of dMRI without clean references—and presents a cohesive pipeline that is practically motivated and empirically strong on multiple downstream metrics. The Fusion process, “Di-” noise modeling, latter-step training, and adaptive sampling together constitute a thoughtful design, and the clinical relevance of the evaluations (tractography, microstructure R²) is a notable strength. However, the theoretical grounding of the training objective is not fully convincing for dMRI: x and x′ come from different diffusion directions, so the Noise2Noise equivalence to supervised MSE is not generally valid without stronger assumptions. Similarly, using x−x′ to derive a noise distribution raises potential conflation of signal and noise. These concerns are somewhat mitigated by extensive ablations and strong empirical results, but a top-tier venue would benefit from either (i) a more rigorous treatment of these assumptions or (ii) expanded empirical analyses demonstrating lack of bias in direction-dependent contrasts (e.g., repeated-direction acquisitions, consistency checks). On balance, I find the work promising and practically valuable, with clear advances over prevalent self-supervised dMRI denoisers and DDM2. With clarifications and strengthened justification, it would be a solid contribution. I lean toward a weak accept.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_074.assessment:
This paper offers a clean theoretical reframing and a practically impactful algorithm for distribution-preserving noise warping. The Brownian-bridge limit is elegant, the resulting algorithm is simple and fast, and the two partition-building variants address both exactness (grid) and robustness/extendability (particle). Empirical validation of Gaussianity, convergence, and significant efficiency gains over HIWYN is convincing, and the downstream demonstrations are promising. The main areas for strengthening are a broader experimental comparison to other training-free consistency methods, deeper analysis under non-injective flows (the most realistic setting), and clarifications around ordering/complexity. Overall, the contribution is technically sound, novel, and valuable to the community; I recommend acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_080.assessment:
This paper delivers a significant engineering milestone: a practical, open 7B model family that processes up to one million tokens across text and video, together with a clear recipe (curriculum, RoPE scaling, masked packing) and strong systems work (RingAttention fused with FlashAttention) to make million-length training feasible. The empirical evidence convincingly shows preserved short-context performance, excellent synthetic retrieval to 1M, and tangible gains on long-video understanding. However, the work’s novelty is primarily integrative and systems-oriented rather than algorithmically new, and parts of the evaluation emphasize synthetic setups with limited coverage of realistic, retrieval-shaped or agentic scenarios. Some methodological details (masking, loss weighting, hyperparameters, hardware/parallelism specifics) need clarification for full reproducibility, and comparisons to recent alternative long-context paradigms and multimodal benchmarks could be expanded.
Overall, I view this as a valuable contribution with high practical impact, especially given the open release. With clarifications and additional evaluations (e.g., MMLongBench, HaystackCraft, RAG vs long-context baselines), the paper would be stronger. I lean toward a weak accept for ICLR, primarily on the basis of systems significance, open-source value, and the demonstration of new long-horizon multimodal capabilities at 1M tokens.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_086.assessment:
Frag2Seq offers an intriguing and practical pathway to bring language models into pocket-conditioned 3D ligand generation by designing an SE(3)-aware fragment tokenization and an LM with cross-attention to protein embeddings. Empirically, it achieves competitive to superior docking proxies and drug-likeness while being much faster than many classical diffusion/AR baselines; the distributional analyses also suggest improved geometric realism. However, the paper overreaches in its theoretical claims (equating canonical SMILES ordering with 3D isomorphism), relies on post hoc bond inference that weakens chemical control, and omits key validity and physical diagnostics. Important recent baselines emphasizing efficiency and fragment-level modeling are not compared, and several implementation details (protein conditioning, degeneracy handling, runtime protocol) need clarification.
Overall, this is a promising direction with solid empirical signals and clear practical appeal. With corrected/tempered theory, expanded diagnostics, clearer conditioning details, and stronger comparisons to recent fast SBDD models, this work could make a valuable ICLR contribution. In its current form I view it as borderline, leaning toward acceptance if the authors address the theoretical overclaims and provide the missing evaluations and context.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_092.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and timely problem—geometry-aware RL for manipulation with varying shapes and deformable objects—through a well-motivated combination of SE(3) equivariance and explicit heterogeneity in a lightweight graph policy. The proposed benchmark is a valuable contribution on its own, and the empirical results are comprehensive and convincing: HEPi consistently outperforms Transformer and homogeneous EMPN baselines, particularly in 3D and multi-actuator/deformable settings, and TRPL brings stable on-policy learning. The theoretical component is modest, and several experimental gaps remain (compute/param fairness, stronger equivariant baselines, success metrics, and real-robot trials). Nonetheless, the overall package—method + benchmark + solid empirical evidence—should be of high interest to the ICLR community. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on clarifications and added details suggested above; even without real-robot evaluation, the benchmark and clear empirical story make a strong case.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_098.assessment:
This paper presents a well-executed, device-aware hybrid backbone that achieves strong accuracy–latency trade-offs on mobile hardware by combining a streamlined ConvNeXt front-end with a simple Single-Head Modulation Attention. The contributions are largely pragmatic—guided by careful profiling and ablations—and the empirical results on classification and dense prediction tasks are convincing and useful for practitioners. The main novelty is moderate: single-head attention is known, and modulation/gating mechanisms have prior art; the combination here is thoughtfully engineered but not conceptually groundbreaking. The paper would benefit from deeper operator-level profiling to substantiate its central latency claims, stronger matched-setting comparisons to key contemporaries (including recent gated/softmax-free mobile hybrids), and clearer module specification. Overall, I find the work solid and practically valuable for the ICLR community, with room to tighten the empirical comparisons and clarity. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on addressing the profiling details, adding stronger baselines under matched settings, and clarifying SHMA’s exact formulation.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_004.assessment:
This paper introduces a compelling and practically meaningful approach to few-shot state classification by explicitly encoding predicate hierarchies in a hyperbolic image–predicate latent space, guided by language-derived pairwise relations. The methodology is well-motivated, the architecture is coherent, and the experimental results—especially on OOD and sim-to-real transfer—are strong with informative ablations. The primary weaknesses are the reliance on LLM-derived supervision (framed as “self-supervised”), limited reporting on statistical variability and hyperbolic design choices, and some missing controls to isolate the effect of correct hierarchical priors. Despite these, the paper provides a significant and novel contribution relevant to ICLR’s audience at the intersection of representation learning, multimodal reasoning, and robotics. With clarifications and additional analyses addressing robustness and fairness, I would lean towards acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_010.assessment:
The paper addresses an important and timely problem—learning stable, neuron-intrinsic representations from dynamics—and proposes a simple VICReg-based formulation with segment-level positives, plus the integration of “surrounding information.” The multi-dataset evaluation and some reported gains are encouraging. However, the current manuscript has significant clarity and methodological issues that undermine confidence in the claims. Chief concerns include the unclear and arguably inappropriate use of CEBRA purely as an SI compressor without specification of training; mismatches between the stated contrastive design and VICReg’s no-negatives implementation; potential confounds introduced by SI (especially session identity) without ablations; and an “out-of-domain” protocol that pretrains on all animals, weakening the core generalization claim. Moreover, several important ablations, fair baseline configurations, and standard identity-evaluation metrics are missing, and related work positioning could be improved.
With substantial revisions—clearer methodological specification, fair and thorough baselines (with/without SI), strict held-out animal pretraining, and identity-oriented diagnostics—the idea could mature into a useful contribution. As it stands, I do not recommend acceptance at ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_016.assessment:
This paper tackles an important, underappreciated consequence of current preference-learning practice: the coupling of entropy and cross-entropy in KL regularization drives majority amplification, reducing diversity and hurting calibration. SPL is a simple, well-motivated fix with clear theoretical underpinnings in a tractable setting and a compelling global-temperature interpretation. The empirical results, while limited in scale, are consistent and practically meaningful—especially the best-of-N gains on hard problems and improved MCQ calibration with minimal accuracy loss. The main reservations are moderate novelty (decoupled entropy/cross-entropy is known in max-ent RL and close to recent decoupled PPO variants), reliance on LLM judges, incomplete baselines (notably missing f-divergences, MaxMin, APO), and the narrowness of the theoretical setting. Overall, I find the work valuable and likely impactful for the community due to its practical simplicity and the salience of the problem it addresses. I lean toward acceptance provided the authors temper claims, expand baselines, and clarify derivations; even as an incremental methodological step, SPL brings a clear, evidenced benefit to a problem that materially affects downstream users and evaluation regimes.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_032.assessment:
This paper advances a timely and practically relevant perspective: in finite-sample regimes, likelihood-ratio importance weights can be too aggressive, and a principled bilevel optimization over group weights can improve the bias–variance trade-off and downstream generalization under sub-population shift. The linear-regression analysis is insightful and the proposed wrapper is versatile—improving several widely used group-robust methods in last-layer retraining pipelines. The empirical evidence is promising, especially on smaller datasets and minority groups, and the qualitative robustness to hyperparameters is appealing.
However, the conceptual novelty is moderate: bilevel reweighting via validation loss has precedents (e.g., Ren et al., 2018), and the present contribution lies in reframing it specifically for group-wise sub-population shift with a clear bias–variance rationale and demonstrating gains across methods. The work would be strengthened by addressing technical gaps (non-smooth hypergradients), expanding comparisons (AFR/SELF/LFR, and a simple shrinkage baseline), providing runtime/convergence details, and clarifying behavior when target group proportions are unknown. Despite these limitations, the paper’s message is important and actionable, and the proposed approach appears to be a practical improvement with minimal integration burden. I lean toward a weak accept.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_014.assessment:
MMAU is a timely and valuable benchmark that fills a notable gap in evaluating audio-grounded reasoning across speech, sound, and music. The dataset is thoughtfully designed, the task coverage is unusually broad, and the empirical study is extensive enough to substantiate the headline claims: current LALMs remain far from human-level performance, with perceptual grounding being the primary bottleneck. The analysis (noise ablations, caption cascades, error taxonomy) provides actionable insights for researchers. There are, however, several areas for improvement before publication: reconcile inconsistent claims in the results section, expand discussion of recent related work and newer baselines, report annotation reliability and potential train–test overlap, and provide statistical uncertainty. These are largely fixable and do not undermine the central contribution. On balance, I find MMAU to be a significant and high-impact resource for the community, suitable for ICLR pending revisions that strengthen methodological transparency and contextualization.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_020.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and surprisingly under-specified question in large-scale LLM training: how to transfer learning rates across token horizons. The empirical evidence that the optimal LR decreases with horizon and follows a robust power law is strong within the studied setup, and the proposed joint law LR*(N,D)=C·N^(-α)·D^(-β) is practically valuable. The work is well-executed, clearly presented, and includes helpful uncertainty quantification and a realistic practitioner rule-of-thumb.
However, two issues temper enthusiasm. First, the generality of β≈0.32 across optimizers, schedules, and larger horizons remains to be demonstrated, and current experiments probe only a subset of influential axes. Second—and most critically—there exists recent empirical work (Step Law) reporting the opposite sign for the D exponent under a specific (but common) cosine-to-fixed-floor schedule; this discrepancy needs to be reconciled via controlled schedule/floor ablations. On the positive side, HyperP independently finds β≈0.32, which supports the present findings and suggests the sign disagreement may be schedule- or metric-induced.
Overall, I view this as a valuable and timely contribution with clear practical implications and a solid experimental backbone. With added schedule/optimizer ablations to address conflicting reports and a more cautious framing of the LLaMA-1 claim (with uncertainty quantification), this paper would make a strong addition to ICLR. I recommend acceptance after addressing the noted gaps.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_044.assessment:
This paper offers a clear and timely data-centric viewpoint on weak-to-strong generalization centered on “overlap density,” along with simple heuristics to detect overlaps and select data sources. The conceptual contribution is appealing, and the synthetic and real-data trends are suggestive. However, the theoretical development rests on strong and somewhat unrealistic assumptions, and the main bound does not deliver a new quantitative relationship between overlap density and generalization beyond reusing the expansion lens. Empirically, the most actionable component—the multi-source selection policy—lacks validation on real, heterogeneous sources, and overlap detection is not compared against strong alternative selection/pruning baselines. Important implementation details (representations, thresholding) are also under-specified. Overall, I view this as an interesting and potentially impactful direction that requires stronger empirical substantiation and tighter theory-to-metric linkage to meet the bar for ICLR. I recommend rejection in the current form, with encouragement to (a) add real multi-source experiments and stronger baselines, (b) provide more robust detection analyses and implementation details, and (c) better quantify how measured overlap maps to expansion and downstream gains.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_008.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and underexplored practical problem: mitigating exaggerated refusals without eroding safety or incurring inference-time costs. The central idea—extracting false and true refusal directions and orthogonalizing to disentangle them—is simple, well-motivated, and empirically effective across several models and datasets. The λ knob provides a particularly compelling affordance for post-training safety calibration. While the approach is incremental relative to existing representation-editing work, it meaningfully advances the state of practice for this safety-use case.
That said, the evaluation could be strengthened. Some harmful-compliance regressions are visible; the XSTest filtering strategy may bias cross-model comparisons; the SCAN head-to-head is not fully apples-to-apples; and statistical uncertainty and per-category analyses are missing. Additional experiments on adversarial robustness, multi-direction (subspace) orthogonalization, and broader human evaluation would further solidify the claims.
Overall, I find the paper to be a valuable, deployable contribution with a clear safety benefit and good engineering intuition. With clarifications and expanded evaluation as suggested, it would be a strong fit for ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_002.assessment:
This paper makes a timely and practical contribution: a clean data formulation that converts BC trajectories into conversational instruction tuning data for VLMs, paired with a thoughtful suite of self-supervised auxiliary datasets. The empirical results convincingly demonstrate strong data efficiency in simulation and meaningful transfer to real tasks with minimal finetuning, and the analysis of auxiliary data scaling is particularly useful. The main limitations are the 2D-only action interface, reliance on external detection and textual numeric outputs, and missing comparisons to a few closely related VLAs; the real-world evaluation scope is also limited. However, the approach is simple, reproducible, and likely to influence how groups adapt general-purpose VLMs into VLA policies under scarce data. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on clarifications and, if feasible, additional baseline comparisons or robustness analyses. The work’s data-centric perspective and auxiliary-dataset construction are valuable to the ICLR community.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

accepted_038.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and practical gap: intuitive, geometry-changing, point-handle-driven editing of real 3D scenes. Its main idea—coarse, explicit 3D deformation as structural scaffolding followed by diffusion-based multi-view correction—is sound and, in the presented results, effective across diverse edits while being reasonably fast. The system-level contributions (copy-and-paste deformation with local masks, progressive scheduling with handle relocation and history-aware fine-tuning) are thoughtfully engineered and easy to adopt.
The main reservation lies in evaluation and contextualization. While qualitative results are strong and user/GPT studies are positive, the work lacks comparisons to the most relevant recent baselines (notably DragScene, as well as multi-view-consistent editors like CoreEditor/TrAME) and does not provide objective 3D-consistency metrics. Furthermore, several methodological choices (deformation parameter sensitivity, opacity/feature handling for duplicated Gaussians) would benefit from deeper analysis. Despite these concerns, I find the contribution valuable and practically impactful. With stronger comparisons, quantitative consistency metrics, and additional analysis of robustness and parameter choices, this work would be a strong addition to ICLR. I lean toward acceptance after addressing these points.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_022.assessment:
This paper presents a pragmatic and effective tweak to LoRA: treat rank‑1 factors as “experts” and apply expert-level stochastic masking during training. The method is refreshingly simple, cost-neutral at inference, and empirically effective on VTAB‑1k and FGVC, with meaningful gains in the hard Structured domain. The analyses on parameter similarity bolster the central intuition that expert-level dropout increases diversity among low-rank components. On the downside, the conceptual novelty is modest (the decomposition is an identity; the core is dropout placement), and some comparisons are missing or underdeveloped (rank-dropout baseline, MoE‑LoRA, PACE). The SOTA claim should be nuanced in light of recent results like PACE. Despite these caveats, the work is of clear practical value: it advances the LoRA toolkit with a minimal change that many practitioners can adopt, and it stimulates further research on where and how to regularize PEFT. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on (i) tempering claims, (ii) adding the missing baselines (especially rank-dropout and, if possible, MoE‑LoRA), and (iii) clarifying hyperparameter tuning fairness.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_040.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and under-explored gap: measuring multi-turn inductive reasoning where models must actively gather evidence and then generalize. The design is elegant and the analyses reveal meaningful, practice-relevant behavior differences (test design vs. inversion skill; doom loops; over-complex guesses). However, the current benchmark scale is limited, the claim of memorization robustness is not yet convincingly supported for a fixed 50-rule set, several key methodological details are under-specified (hypothesis-reduction grammar, decoding parameters), and highly relevant related work on interactive program/function inference (notably CodeARC) is missing from comparisons. With strengthened methodology (multi-seed runs, baseline algorithms, parameter disclosures), expanded/hidden/procedural test suites, and deeper positioning relative to closely related interactive benchmarks, WILT could become a valuable community resource. As it stands, I view this as a promising benchmark paper with clear insights but in need of additional rigor and scope to meet ICLR’s bar.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_058.assessment:
This paper is timely and relevant: bringing Mamba-2 into online model-based RL is a plausible and useful step toward compute- and parameter-efficient agents, and the reported Atari-100k results with a 7M world model are encouraging. The DFS heuristic is simple and appears beneficial in mean performance, and the ablations provide some support for Mamba-2’s efficiency advantages over Mamba. However, the contribution is largely incremental relative to concurrent SSM world-model work (R2I, S5WM) and more recent Mamba-based world models (e.g., GLAM, EDELINE). The paper under-cites these and overstates novelty. Empirically, the evaluation lacks robust statistical reporting and stronger sampling baselines; several methodological details are ambiguous, and the DreamerV3-XS baseline likely underestimates a tuned small-scale Dreamer. Overall, I find the direction promising and the early results positive, but the paper falls short of ICLR standards in novelty positioning, rigor of comparisons, and clarity/reproducibility. I lean toward rejection in its current form, with the view that a revised version incorporating broader baselines, stronger statistical reporting, clearer architectural/protocol details, and contextualized comparisons to contemporaneous SSM/Mamba world models would be competitive.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_034.assessment:
This paper presents a simple yet powerful idea: perform adversarial purification in CLIP’s multimodal latent space, using either a diffusion prior or, more strikingly, a discriminative cosine “likelihood” to guide a fast, unit-normalized embedding update. The theoretical SDE-based risk analysis provides a plausible motivation, and the empirical results—if they hold under fully adaptive evaluation—are exceptionally strong, with notable efficiency gains for CLIPure-Cos. That said, the extraordinary ImageNet robustness numbers mandate stringent adaptive stress-testing and clearer apples-to-apples baselines. The likelihood interpretation for the cosine score is heuristic and would benefit from deeper justification or calibration analyses. The related work comparison should include recent non-generative, training-free defenses (TTC, CSR) and emerging multimodal purification approaches (e.g., DiffCAP).
Overall, I view the conceptual contribution and practical promise as significant. With reinforced adaptive evaluation, clarified experimental protocol, and broader comparisons, this work would be a valuable addition to ICLR. I lean toward acceptance contingent on addressing the adaptive-attack and comparison concerns.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_064.assessment:
This paper presents a clean and practically impactful idea: learn a value function off-policy with a WIS-weighted TD loss and pair it with a strong trust-region policy update, thereby avoiding the complexity and fragility of Q-learning in high-dimensional action spaces. The theoretical contributions (upper bound via Jensen, bandit variance intuition) are modest but correct and help motivate the estimator choice. Empirically, the results on high-dimensional control are compelling, with careful ablations and statistically sound reporting. The main weakness is the limited set of baselines relative to the current state of the art in high-dimensional off-policy RL (e.g., FlashSAC, AFU, Q3C, QFLEX). Strengthening empirical comparisons and clarifying efficiency claims would meaningfully improve the paper. Overall, I find the approach interesting, relevant, and potentially useful for the community; with stronger contextualization and additional baselines, I would be inclined toward acceptance. As it stands, I lean toward a weak accept contingent on addressing the baseline and efficiency reporting gaps.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_052.assessment:
This paper addresses an important problem—scalable, label-free self-alignment for multi-turn tool-using dialogue agents—and presents a practical, effective solution. JOSH is conceptually simple, easy to implement, and yields strong gains on two relevant benchmarks while maintaining general capabilities. The introduction of ToolWOZ as an API-grounded, sparse-reward benchmark is itself useful to the community. The main limitations are its reliance on simulator-based evaluation, a relatively weak set of alternative training baselines (particularly for offline RL and preference optimization variants), and limited ablations on key design knobs. Despite these, the empirical improvements across model scales and prompts are compelling, and the paper’s contributions—both methodological and infrastructural—are likely to be valuable. I recommend a weak accept, contingent on clarifications (AR definition, partial-credit usage) and, ideally, additional ablations or stronger baselines in the camera-ready.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_046.assessment:
This is a solid and timely contribution that operationalizes activation steering for practical, fine-grained instruction adherence. The paper convincingly shows that simple difference-in-means vectors, applied at a single layer, can improve compliance with format and length constraints and modulate keyword inclusion/exclusion—even when instructions are already present—while enabling multi-constraint composition and notable cross-model transfer from instruction-tuned to base models. The approach is easy to reproduce and deploy where activations are accessible.
The main limitations are the relatively narrow benchmarking scope (IFEval subsets and synthetic prompts), limited comparisons to alternative controllability baselines (constrained decoding, logit-bias), and an incomplete quantification of side effects (fluency degradation, safety interactions, robustness under sampling). These are addressable in revisions. Given the pragmatic value, strong empirical signals, and the novelty of cross-model transfer for instruction adhering vectors, I recommend acceptance after encouraging the authors to add stronger baseline comparisons and deeper robustness/side-effect analyses.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_028.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and understudied aspect of learning on directed graphs: capturing mutual, asymmetric relationships beyond unidirectional shortest paths. The proposal to integrate commute-time-based proximities into a direction-aware message-passing scheme is novel and experimentally compelling, especially on heterophilic web/social datasets where mutual reachability better reflects interaction strength. The introduction of a DiLap operator and the claimed equivalence between its pseudoinverse and the fundamental matrix, if rigorously correct, would represent a meaningful theoretical contribution. However, the current presentation leaves several technical ambiguities (notation, exactness conditions, symmetry vs. asymmetry) and practical concerns (quadratic memory to materialize C, incomplete efficiency accounting) unresolved. The experimental section is strong in breadth but would be significantly strengthened by comparisons to recent rewiring methods and by clearer reporting of preprocessing costs and memory. Overall, I view the work as promising and potentially impactful; with tightened theory, clarified algorithmic details, and stronger baselines/scalability evidence, it would be well-suited for ICLR. In its current form, I lean toward a weak accept contingent on addressing the main theoretical and scalability clarifications.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_070.assessment:
This paper addresses an important question—automating neighborhood scope in GNNs—via a principled Bayesian nonparametric approach that is generic across backbones. The conceptual framing is elegant; the empirical results are broadly positive and include a compelling PPI case study and improvements in uncertainty calibration. The expressivity analysis is directionally consistent with the empirical observations, though it would benefit from clearer assumptions and operational definitions in the main text.
However, the novelty is incremental relative to prior depth/dropout inference in neural networks, now interpreted for GNN hops, and the method’s adaptivity is global rather than node-wise. The experimental study would be significantly strengthened by comparisons against closely related adaptive multi-hop and node-adaptive baselines (APPNP/CAPGNN/ScaleGNN, NAI, MetSelect) under matched protocols; by clarifying splits, tuning fairness, and sparse complexity; and by expanding calibration metrics. As it stands, I view the paper as promising and practically useful, but with notable gaps in positioning and evaluation. With these addressed, it would make a solid contribution. My current stance is borderline accept.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_076.assessment:
This paper presents a clean, theoretically grounded, and practically motivated approach to detoxification that leverages a learned linear subspace in the model’s own embedding space to steer decoding without auxiliary models or retraining. The empirical results are promising—often improving on a strong baseline (RAD) across several datasets and models—while maintaining reasonable fluency and cost. However, there are important omissions that limit confidence at the ICLR bar: (i) insufficient implementation details and analysis for the subspace learner (covariance estimation) and for efficient per-token margin computation at inference; (ii) a serious evaluator-alignment concern due to using a Jigsaw-derived dataset for learning and Perspective API for evaluation, without orthogonal metrics or human studies; and (iii) missing discussion and comparisons to several closely related 2023–2024 training-free steering approaches (activation- and logit-space), which are critical to situate novelty and practicality. I view the core idea as solid and impactful, but the paper needs additional technical clarity and stronger, more comprehensive evaluation to be fully convincing. With revisions addressing the above, SASA could be a valuable contribution to inference-time alignment. As it stands, I lean toward a borderline acceptance; my recommendation depends on the authors’ ability to clarify the technical pipeline and add evaluator-robust evidence.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_094.assessment:
This paper presents a thoughtfully engineered and well-motivated extension to visual MBRL for open-world tasks. The integration of an affordance-guided jumpy imagination branch with Dreamer-style short-horizon rollouts is novel and addresses a salient limitation—short-sighted value propagation and inefficient exploration in sparsely rewarded, pixel-only settings. The empirical results, though limited in scope, are consistently positive and supported by useful ablations and qualitative analyses. On the downside, the method relies on heuristic components (kurtosis-based jump trigger), external VLM rewards (MineCLIP), and a nontrivial preprocessing pipeline (U‑Net distillation) whose compute cost and generality across tasks are not fully quantified. Some modeling and reporting details are under-specified, and broader comparisons to recent long-horizon/optimistic MBRL are absent. Overall, I view LS-Imagine as a promising contribution that should be of interest to ICLR’s MBRL and embodied AI communities. With clarifications, more comprehensive statistics, and additional comparisons, I would lean toward acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_082.assessment:
RocketEval is a well-motivated, practical framework that pushes the idea of training-free, checklist-guided lightweight judges forward with a thoughtful decomposition and a simple, effective normalization scheme. The empirical gains—especially making 2–8B judges competitive with expensive proprietary models for ranking—are potentially impactful for the community’s day-to-day evaluation needs. However, the paper’s novelty is moderate relative to concurrent checklist/rubric-based judging frameworks, and several important methodological details and comparisons are missing. Most critically, the supervised predictor setup risks leakage without clear train/test splits; the α_r weighting is under-specified; and robustness analyses and head-to-heads with recent checklist methods are needed to substantiate broad claims of reliability and reproducibility. With stronger baselines (CheckEval/CE-Judge/AutoChecklist), clearer supervised-training protocols (cross-validation), item-length normalization, and robustness/stability reporting (e.g., scoring-bias perturbations, confidence intervals), this work could become a valuable contribution. As it stands, I view it as promising but not yet at the bar for ICLR; I encourage a revision addressing the concerns above.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_006.assessment:
This paper offers a thoughtful, practice-driven adaptation of online policy selection to realistic inventory systems, with two particularly valuable contributions: (i) a clean OPS embedding that covers perishability, lead times, and capacity coupling, and (ii) a careful treatment of nonsmoothness via one-sided derivatives that addresses a real failure mode of autodiff in online control. Empirically, GAPSI is competitive and often superior to MPC and simple base-stock baselines on M5 and a proprietary dataset, with strong runtime advantages.
However, for a top-tier venue, the paper falls short on two fronts. First, there is no theoretical guarantee (regret, stability, or even a principled justification for the derivative selection), despite explicitly violating the key OPS assumptions; at minimum, partial guarantees in a simplified setting (e.g., L=0, no capacity) or a principled calculus-based selection rule would strengthen the contribution. Second, the experimental scope omits lead-time evaluations and stronger modern baselines (nonstationary base-stock learners, DRL/OR-guided systems), and several ablations are missing (buffer size B, AdaGrad schedule, capacity penalty sensitivity, censored-demand evaluation).
Overall, I view this as a promising and practically useful contribution with novel insights, but currently better suited as a workshop paper or for revision. With added theory (even partial) and expanded experiments/baselines, it could become a compelling ICLR submission.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

random_088.assessment:
This paper presents a compelling, well-executed approach to program synthesis via iterative, grammar-constrained edits guided by rendered outputs and a learned value model. The core idea—framing synthesis as edit denoising over syntax trees—fits inverse graphics especially well and is convincingly validated on two DSLs with clear gains over autoregressive and REPL-style baselines (where applicable). The methodology is thoughtfully engineered (reverse-path supervision, grammar-constrained decoding) and the ablations substantiate key design choices. The primary limitations are the narrow domain scope (simple DSLs with discretized parameters), somewhat asymmetric baseline coverage (no REPL in TinySVG, limited comparisons to alternative search paradigms), and the looser connection to formal diffusion modeling. Nonetheless, the work opens a promising direction for structured synthesis that leverages execution feedback without partial compilers. I recommend a weak accept: the contributions are novel and practically useful, the empirical evidence is solid within scope, and the paper is clearly written with honest discussion of limitations.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_012.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and well-motivated problem: LLMs’ tendency to exploit spurious correlations by relying on only one part of a paired input. The proposed TC method is simple, practical, and generally effective, delivering consistent improvements across many datasets, models, and prompts, with additional benefits in few-shot scenarios and complementarity with other calibration methods. The main reservation is the theoretical grounding: the jump from a mutual information decomposition to the specific instance-level scoring introduces heuristic choices that are not fully justified, and the fixed weights are not ablated. The presentation also has minor polish issues and could improve with more robust significance reporting and runtime analysis. Overall, I view TC as a valuable and broadly applicable inference-time tool; with clarified derivations and additional ablations, it would be a strong contribution. I lean toward a weak accept.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_018.assessment:
This paper offers a clear and appealing interpretation of attention as subspace denoising and proposes a minimal attention-only architecture obtained by unrolling this operator. The conceptual clarity and the multi-domain empirical evaluation are strengths, and the language modeling results are competitive with GPT-2-base at similar parameter scales. However, the theoretical guarantee rests on restrictive assumptions and a nonstandard attention rule, and there is a notable mismatch between the theorem and practice (thresholded-softmax vs. softmax, orthogonality, and the “linear rate” inconsistency). Empirically, the “close to standard” claim holds more on language tasks than on vision; the ImageNet gap is sizable, and ablations probing the architecture’s stability and learned subspaces are limited. Overall, the work advances the “white-box” transformer agenda and is of interest to ICLR, but in its present form it feels like a solid step with moderate novelty rather than a definitive advance. I would encourage a revision that (i) reconciles the rate claims and strengthens the theory-practice bridge, (ii) expands ablations and fairness details, and (iii) deepens interpretability analyses. My recommendation is borderline reject, with enthusiasm for a stronger, better-substantiated revision.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_000.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and practical shortcoming of single-image NVS: misalignment between desired and generated poses/appearances under standard diffusion score-matching training. The proposed solution—closed-loop feature alignment using CLIP patch features combined with an alternating training schedule—yields consistent improvements on pose-consistency metrics (AA, IoU) and modest but meaningful gains in image quality. The method is simple to implement in existing pipelines and the empirical results are convincing overall. The main weaknesses are modest conceptual novelty beyond perceptual-loss-based finetuning, reliance on AA measured by a pose estimator with CAD dependency (especially for symmetric/ambiguous cases), and the lack of quantitative 3D metrics. Despite these, the paper’s contribution is valuable to the community: a practical training strategy that improves a widely used baseline (Zero123) in a way that matters for downstream 3D. I lean toward acceptance, contingent on clarifying the evaluation choices and adding (even in the camera-ready) quantitative 3D measures and more discussion of symmetry and baseline parity.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_024.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and somewhat under-explored part of ViTs—the FFN/channel mixer—and presents a well-motivated, practical method that consistently improves accuracy/efficiency trade-offs. The combination of a block-diagonal MLP (to enlarge expansion within fixed compute) and a training-only, parameter-free channel covariance attention (to regularize and enable inter-group communication) is conceptually clean and empirically effective. The breadth of experiments across tasks and backbones, together with ablations and analysis of the learned α weights and feature separability, provides convincing support for the main claims. The principal reservations concern the degree of novelty (largely an effective re-assembly of known building blocks), the fairness and completeness of throughput comparisons, and limited discussion/contrast with strong ViT+MoE baselines and XCiT-like channel attention variants. Overall, I find the contribution useful and sufficiently validated for ICLR, particularly due to its plug-and-play nature, zero inference overhead for the auxiliary branch, and clear deployment relevance. I lean toward acceptance, and encourage the authors to bolster the empirical comparisons and related-work positioning to maximize impact.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_030.assessment:
This paper makes a timely and useful contribution by defining OCGL, articulating its constraints, and assembling a benchmark with appropriate anytime metrics. The diagnosis of neighborhood expansion and the insistence on bounded per-update compute are important for the community. The empirical study is broad and the proposed LINEAR baseline is a strong, practical reference that often matches or outperforms more complex GNN-based methods under the imposed constraints. The main drawbacks are limited algorithmic novelty beyond setting formalization and sampling, absence of system-level efficiency measurements, and incomplete comparisons to scalable/temporal GNN baselines or heterophily-aware architectures. Despite these gaps, the work is valuable for framing and standardizing research in online graph learning and for the insight that simple, topology-aware replay and light models can be highly competitive under strict online constraints. I lean toward acceptance contingent on clarifying leakage/stream mechanics and adding at least some efficiency reporting and a stronger baseline (e.g., GraphSAGE with neighbor sampling); even without, the benchmark and baseline would likely serve the community well.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_036.assessment:
This paper addresses an important problem—test-time alignment/personalization under task underspecification—through a simple, practical idea: reweighting a diverse ensemble using a tiny labeled adaptation set. Despite the apparent simplicity and the strong connections to classical exponential weighting (Hedge/EWA) and generalized Bayes, the paper’s contribution lies in demonstrating that, with modern efficient ensemble parameterizations, this strategy scales to large pretrained models and can yield substantial real-world gains, particularly in preference personalization of LLM reward models. The empirical scope is broad and the results on low-shot adaptation are compelling. However, the novelty claims should be tempered by acknowledging established theory on expert aggregation, and the experimental section would be stronger with (i) additional competitive baselines in the LLM setting (LoRA/ReFT), (ii) a hard-select-one-head baseline, and (iii) more thorough ablations/diagnostics on diversity and sensitivity to noise. Overall, I find the work valuable and likely to have practical impact; with clarifications and a few targeted additions, it would meet the bar for ICLR.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_042.assessment:
This paper proposes a genuinely interesting and feasible block coordinate approach for nonsmooth composite optimization under orthogonality constraints, with a clear orthogonality-preserving update, a principled majorization scheme, and a useful block-k stationarity concept. The k = 2 breakpoint solver is a practical contribution that seems to work well for L0–SPCA. The theoretical analysis (sufficient decrease, O(1/ε) ergodic, KL-based last-iterate rates) is solid under stated assumptions and helps position the method conceptually. However, the work has important gaps: the assumption of exact subproblem solves for general k and nonsmooth h is strong; the Riemannian subgradient analysis does not cover L0; and the empirical evaluation omits key recent baselines that are specifically designed for this problem class. Some novelty claims (basis representation) are overstated. With stronger and broader experiments (including modern manifold methods), a clarified treatment of L0 in the theory (or explicit limitation statements), and practical guidance for k > 2 subproblems, the paper would make a compelling case. In its current form, I see promising ideas that are likely valuable to the community, but the evaluation and some assumptions need tightening for a top-tier venue.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_048.assessment:
This paper addresses an important open gap—tractable optimal transport between probabilistic circuits—by introducing coupling circuits and a recursively computable CWp distance that yields both values and transport plans efficiently. The core idea is elegant, well-motivated, and practically useful, with strong runtime evidence and qualitative applications. The hardness results and the empirical parameter-learning variant further enrich the contribution, even though WM currently lags EM in likelihood and seems to stall for large models.
However, there are issues that should be addressed before publication. Most notably, the metric claim appears misstated: CWp^p is not a metric, and the triangle inequality argument in the appendix needs correction for the p-rooted definition. Additional clarifications on complexity (dependence on branching factors), the exact form of the learning objective in Table 1, and expanded related-work context (OT with graphical/circuit structure; OT-based learning in DAGs) would strengthen the paper. The experimental suite is generally solid for efficiency and proxy utility; the learning experiments are promising but would benefit from ablations (soft routing) and clearer objective definitions.
Overall, I find the conceptual contribution and algorithmic framework novel and valuable to both the PC and OT communities. With the above fixes and clarifications, I recommend acceptance.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_054.assessment:
This work presents an appealing and principled synthesis of pullback geometry and flow matching, with a practical isometry-learning objective and a diffeomorphic parameterization via Neural ODEs. The idea of training flows on a low-dimensional latent manifold with closed-form geodesics while preserving data-manifold geometry is sound and important, and the paper provides promising empirical evidence on interpolation and some generative tasks. However, the experimental evaluation has notable gaps: the absence of direct comparisons to Metric Flow Matching (a very close baseline), limited scalability analysis, and uneven generative results (especially on Swiss roll) weaken the empirical support for the central claims. Some notational and methodological ambiguities (e.g., Eq. (10), where the vector field lives) also hinder clarity. Overall, I view this as a promising direction with solid conceptual underpinnings, but it would benefit from stronger and broader empirical validation, clearer exposition, and sensitivity/scalability analyses. I recommend a weak reject in its current form, with encouragement to address the raised issues; doing so could make this a strong contribution to geometry-aware generative modeling.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_060.assessment:
This paper takes a meaningful step toward practical, scalable data-driven Riemannian geometry by coupling a tractable pullback metric (with closed-form maps under a quadratic ψ) to a tailored normalizing flow training scheme that promotes near-isometry and anisotropy. The theoretical development is clean and the induced RAE with a PCA-like bound is an attractive byproduct that yields interpretable latent spaces and intrinsic dimension estimates. Experiments on synthetic data are convincing; image experiments are promising but limited, and the method’s reliance on unimodality/near-isometry narrows scope relative to fully general settings. The paper would be strengthened by broader empirical comparisons (e.g., Spread Flows, LID estimators), runtime/ablation analyses of regularization, and more discussion on extending beyond unimodality. Overall, I find the contribution original and valuable, with solid theory and a practical algorithmic recipe, and recommend acceptance after addressing the noted empirical and contextual gaps.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_066.assessment:
This paper makes a timely and impactful contribution by unifying scalable stylometry with per-user generative modeling via parameter-efficient multi-task adapters. The experimental results in chess and Rocket League are strong and provide multiple compelling analyses of the learned style space, including composition, interpolation, and interpretable steering. The approach is conceptually clean, computationally efficient, and practically useful. The main reservations concern missing comparisons to closely related, recent chess baselines (Maia-2, Maia4All), limited robustness analyses, and underdeveloped evaluation for the image-editing extension. Additionally, discussing ethical implications is essential given the de-anonymization potential. Overall, I find the core contribution solid and broadly valuable to the ICLR community. With strengthened baselines, additional robustness and behavioral evaluations, and an explicit ethics section, this work deserves publication. I recommend acceptance, leaning toward a weak accept given the missing contemporary chess comparisons and the limited evaluation of the non-gaming application.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_072.assessment:
This paper presents a practically valuable integration of a MaskGIT prior into a transformer world model and demonstrates promising gains, especially on Atari 100k, together with a simple and effective pathway to handle continuous actions via a state mixer. The idea is well-motivated, and the evaluation employs modern, statistically grounded RL metrics. However, the technical novelty is moderate given prior use of MaskGIT for visual dynamics and recent concurrent world-model work adopting similar masked decoding. More concerning are missing or insufficiently discussed baselines (notably EMERALD and Simulus), ambiguities in the masking schedule and decoding configuration, and inconsistencies in the DMC reporting. The paper would benefit from tighter comparisons, clearer methodological details (including compute and throughput), and additional ablations quantifying the benefits and costs of masked decoding during imagination. Overall, I view this as a solid and potentially useful contribution with moderate originality; with clarified reporting and stronger positioning against closely related recent work, it could be a good fit. As it stands, my recommendation is borderline leaning toward rejection, primarily due to missing comparisons and reporting clarity, but I encourage resubmission with the above issues addressed.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_078.assessment:
This paper addresses an important and under-explored problem: explaining hypergraph neural networks. SHypX is technically sound, simple, and broadly applicable, producing both local and global explanations without training surrogate attention models. The proposed synthetic benchmarks and generalized fidelity metrics are timely and valuable for the community. Empirically, SHypX delivers strong gains in faithfulness while maintaining concision across a diverse set of datasets, and the global concept visualizations are informative. The main weaknesses are the lack of comparisons to graph-explainer baselines via standard hypergraph expansions, incomplete analysis of runtime/scalability, and limited quantitative evaluation of the global component; there is also some confusion in fidelity notation that should be corrected. Overall, I find the contributions substantive and likely to have impact, especially the benchmarks and the first global hypergraph explainer. I recommend acceptance after addressing the above concerns, with a particular emphasis on clarifying metrics, adding at least one expansion-based baseline, and reporting runtime/stability details.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_084.assessment:
This paper offers an interesting and timely exploration of using sparse autoencoder features to perform interpretable, activation-level unlearning on hazardous biological knowledge. The qualitative case study and the observation that negative clamping can flip answers with small loss changes are valuable findings. The comparison to RMU and the decoder-swap control add useful insight, and the paper is refreshingly candid that current SAE-based unlearning induces larger side effects than RMU on unrelated multiple-choice tasks.
However, for an ICLR main-track standard, the experimental design has important shortcomings. The side-effect analysis on MMLU is biased by selective category removal; reliance on multiple-choice WMDP-bio with minimal worst-case or semantic-judge evaluation weakens the evidence; and there is limited statistical rigor and generalization analysis across paraphrases or mixed-query settings. The approach is also a suppression mechanism rather than true weight-level unlearning, and broader baselines (JSD-based, Unilogit, PERMU, span/concept-level methods) and stronger robustness diagnostics (UNPACT, SEPS) are missing.
Overall, I see this as a promising workshop-level contribution that surfaces both the potential and limitations of SAE-driven interventions for safety. To meet ICLR standards, I recommend substantial strengthening of evaluation (unbiased side-effect metrics, worst-case paraphrase and recoverability tests, mixed-query robustness), clearer ablations for the decoder-swap control, broader baselines, and more systematic coverage of SAE design choices. My recommendation is reject for ICLR, with encouragement to pursue this line in a more comprehensive follow-up.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_090.assessment:
This work addresses an important and timely challenge: evaluating the factuality of LMs in real, evolving usage contexts. The two core contributions—VERIFY and FACTBENCH—are thoughtfully designed and, taken together, constitute a meaningful step beyond static, narrow benchmarks and overly binary factuality judgments. The undecidable label, evidence-first design, and in-the-wild prompt mining are clear advances that align better with human reasoning under uncertainty. The empirical findings are informative, especially the nuanced analysis of undecidable content and refusal behavior.
The main reservations concern methodological robustness and fairness: dependence on a single judge LM across multiple pipeline stages, modest human meta-evaluation scale with moderate agreement, lack of VeriScore in comparisons, and baseline judge inconsistencies. The hallucination score requires stronger sensitivity analyses, and the “dynamic” aspect, while compelling, is not yet demonstrated in a released updated version.
Despite these caveats, the paper’s contributions are substantial and practically valuable. With added ablations, more controlled baseline comparisons, and clearer reproducibility measures (evidence caching, authority modeling), this work would be a strong reference for community-wide, ongoing factuality assessment. I lean toward acceptance given the novelty, practicality, and clear potential for impact, contingent on addressing the noted experimental and methodological gaps.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review

rejected_096.assessment:
This paper presents a compelling and practically relevant direction: specialize a powerful T2I prior for face detail and couple it with a restoration adapter plus a time-aware, region-focused loss to enhance authenticity. The qualitative results are persuasive, and the face-oriented IQA gains (especially TOPIQ-FACE) are promising. However, the work would benefit from stronger empirical support on identity preservation, broader and more recent baselines (e.g., PGDiff, CLR-Face, WaveFace), fuller ablations of the Stage I objectives, and clearer technical details for the time-aware discriminator. Given ICLR’s standards, I view the submission as borderline: the idea is solid and of high interest, but the current experimental evidence and comparisons are not yet fully convincing to substantiate the central “authentic, identity-faithful restoration” claim. Strengthening the evaluation with identity metrics, runtime reporting, and additional baselines would likely make this work a strong contender.

                        

                        
                            
                                
                                Full Review
